primary.authors: Chakraborty A, Patel P, Can AS

questions.verifiedExplanation: The FIRST intervention in hyperkalemia with EKG changes is IV calcium gluconate. Calcium does NOT lower potassium — it stabilizes the cardiac membrane by restoring the transmembrane voltage gradient, protecting against lethal arrhythmias. Onset is 1-3 minutes, duration 30-60 minutes. This buys time for potassium-lowering therapies to take effect. StatPearls states: 'Calcium gluconate is generally preferred as the initial agent in patients with evidence of cardiac toxicity.' The treatment sequence is: (1) IV calcium gluconate — membrane stabilization (immediate); (2) IV insulin + glucose — shifts K+ intracellularly (onset 20-30 min); (3) Albuterol nebulization — additional intracellular shift; (4) Kayexalate/patiromer/SZC — potassium elimination (slow onset); (5) Hemodialysis — definitive removal if refractory. Kayexalate, furosemide, and insulin all lower K+ but do NOT protect the heart immediately.
verifiedReferences: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

Chakraborty A, Patel P, Can AS. Calcium Gluconate. [Updated 2024 Feb 28]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK557463/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Calcium gluconate is generally preferred as the initial agent in patients with evidence of cardiac toxicity

supporting.keyFinding: Calcium should promptly be administered to any patient with hyperkalemia and EKG changes... does not resolve the issue of hyperkalemia

questions.verifiedExplanation: The earliest EKG change in hyperkalemia is peaked, narrow T waves (especially in precordial leads V2-V4). This occurs because elevated extracellular K+ accelerates phase 3 repolarization of the action potential. The classic progression of hyperkalemic EKG changes with rising K+ is: (1) Peaked T waves (5.5-6.5 mEq/L); (2) Prolonged PR interval (6.5-7.0); (3) Loss of P waves (7.0-7.5); (4) Widened QRS (7.5-8.0); (5) Sine wave pattern (>8.0); (6) Asystole/VF. ST elevation is associated with MI or pericarditis, not hyperkalemia. Widened QRS is a LATE finding of hyperkalemia, not early. Prolonged QT is associated with hypokalemia and hypocalcemia, not hyperkalemia (hyperkalemia actually shortens the QT interval initially).
verifiedReferences: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: progression of EKG changes: increased T wave amplitude (peaked T waves), prolongation of the PR interval and QRS duration, loss of P waves, culminating in sine wave pattern and asystole

questions.verifiedExplanation: Insulin lowers serum potassium by driving K+ into cells via stimulation of the Na+/K+-ATPase pump on cell membranes. Insulin activates this pump, which moves 2 K+ ions into the cell for every 3 Na+ ions pumped out, shifting potassium from the extracellular to intracellular compartment. A common regimen is 10 units regular insulin IV with 50 mL of D50 (or 25g dextrose) to prevent hypoglycemia, with onset in 20-30 minutes and peak effect at 30-60 minutes. Insulin does NOT chelate potassium (chelation involves binding ions in the blood), does NOT increase renal excretion (that's the mechanism of loop diuretics), and does NOT bind K+ in the GI tract (that's the mechanism of kayexalate/patiromer/SZC). Glucose monitoring is essential post-administration, as hypoglycemia is a common and dangerous complication.
verifiedReferences: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Insulin, with or without glucose in hyperglycemic patients, shifts potassium into cells, lowering serum potassium levels effectively... by increasing Na-K-ATPase activity

questions.verifiedExplanation: Renal failure is the #1 cause of hyperkalemia. The kidneys are responsible for excreting approximately 90% of daily potassium load. When GFR declines below 30 mL/min, potassium excretion becomes impaired, leading to accumulation. StatPearls states: 'Acute and chronic kidney disease are common causes of hyperkalemia. Elevations in serum potassium typically do not occur until the glomerular filtration rate declines below 30 mL/min.' Excessive dietary K+ intake rarely causes hyperkalemia alone in patients with normal renal function. Alkalosis actually causes HYPOkalemia (not hyperkalemia) by shifting K+ INTO cells. Insulin therapy causes hypokalemia, not hyperkalemia — insulin deficiency (as in DKA) causes hyperkalemia.
verifiedReferences: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Acute and chronic kidney disease are common causes of hyperkalemia. Elevations in serum potassium typically do not occur until the glomerular filtration rate declines below 30 mL/min

questions.verifiedExplanation: A sine wave pattern on EKG with K+ >8 mEq/L indicates imminent cardiac arrest and requires emergent treatment. The sine wave represents the final EKG stage before asystole or ventricular fibrillation — the widened QRS merges with the T wave, creating a sinusoidal pattern that indicates loss of effective cardiac conduction. This is a true medical emergency requiring immediate IV calcium gluconate (cardiac membrane stabilization), followed by aggressive potassium-lowering therapies and likely emergent hemodialysis. This is NEVER artifact, NEVER a 'typical finding,' and NEVER appropriate for observation alone. Treatment should not be delayed for repeat testing or confirmatory labs.
verifiedReferences: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: merging of the QRS complex with the T wave producing a sine wave pattern, and asystole

questions.verifiedExplanation: ACE inhibitors/ARBs combined with potassium-sparing diuretics (spironolactone, eplerenone, amiloride, triamterene) are a well-known combination that commonly causes hyperkalemia. ACEi/ARBs reduce aldosterone secretion, decreasing renal K+ excretion. K+-sparing diuretics independently block potassium secretion in the collecting duct. Together, the additive effect on K+ retention creates significant hyperkalemia risk. StatPearls lists 'medications that disrupt potassium homeostasis, such as potassium-sparing diuretics and renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibitors' as key causes. Calcium channel blockers do not significantly affect potassium. Metformin and glipizide do not cause hyperkalemia. Loop diuretics and thiazides cause HYPOkalemia (increased renal K+ excretion), not hyperkalemia.
verifiedReferences: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: medications that disrupt potassium homeostasis, such as potassium-sparing diuretics and renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibitors

questions.verifiedExplanation: Pseudohyperkalemia should be suspected when K+ is elevated but the EKG is normal and the result is clinically unexpected. Pseudohyperkalemia is an in vitro artifact caused by K+ release from cells during or after blood draw — NOT a true elevation of in vivo serum potassium. Common causes include hemolysis during phlebotomy (most common), prolonged tourniquet application, fist clenching during draw, extreme thrombocytosis (>500,000/μL), or leukocytosis (>100,000/μL). The key clinical clue: the patient has no EKG changes and no symptoms consistent with hyperkalemia. Management: redraw the specimen carefully (no tourniquet, no fist clenching, gentle handling) and recheck. Pseudohyperkalemia does NOT require treatment. However, K+ >8 with cardiac arrest is real hyperkalemia requiring emergent treatment, not pseudohyperkalemia.
verifiedReferences: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: A systematic approach to diagnosing hyperkalemia allows clinicians to distinguish true potassium elevation from pseudohyperkalemia

questions.verifiedExplanation: For every 0.1 decrease in blood pH, serum potassium rises approximately 0.6 mEq/L. This occurs because acidosis (excess H+ ions) drives hydrogen ions INTO cells via the H+/K+ exchanger, and potassium moves OUT of cells to maintain electroneutrality. This is particularly relevant in metabolic acidosis (e.g., DKA, lactic acidosis, renal tubular acidosis). The clinical implication: a patient in DKA may have a 'normal' or elevated serum K+ despite massive total body potassium depletion — as acidosis is corrected with insulin and fluids, K+ will shift back into cells and the patient may develop life-threatening hypokalemia. This is why potassium replacement is started early in DKA management even when serum K+ appears normal. Note: this relationship is most predictable with mineral acidoses; organic acidoses (lactic, ketoacidosis) may show a less predictable relationship.
verifiedReferences: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Metabolic acidosis may cause intracellular potassium to shift into the extracellular space

questions.verifiedExplanation: With K+ 7.2, peaked T waves, AND widened QRS, the most critical immediate intervention is IV calcium gluconate for cardiac membrane stabilization. The widened QRS indicates significant cardiac toxicity — this is a medical emergency. Calcium gluconate acts within 1-3 minutes to restore the transmembrane voltage gradient, protecting the myocardium from lethal arrhythmias. It does NOT lower potassium; it buys time. After calcium, insulin + dextrose should follow to shift K+ intracellularly. Sodium bicarbonate alone is less effective and slower. Oral kayexalate takes hours to work and has no role in acute emergent management. The treatment hierarchy in hyperkalemic emergency is: stabilize (calcium) → shift (insulin/glucose, albuterol) → remove (dialysis, diuretics, binders).
verifiedReferences: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Calcium should promptly be administered to any patient with hyperkalemia and EKG changes indicating a hyperkalemic emergency

questions.verifiedExplanation: ACE inhibitors and ARBs are the most common outpatient cause of hyperkalemia. These medications block the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS), reducing aldosterone-mediated potassium secretion in the distal nephron. Risk is highest in patients with concurrent CKD, diabetes, or those taking additional RAAS-blocking agents or potassium-sparing diuretics. Other common outpatient offenders include NSAIDs (reduce renal blood flow and aldosterone), trimethoprim (blocks ENaC in collecting duct), and potassium-sparing diuretics. Loop diuretics and thiazide diuretics cause hypokalemia, not hyperkalemia. Calcium channel blockers have minimal effect on potassium homeostasis.
verifiedReferences: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: medications that disrupt potassium homeostasis, such as potassium-sparing diuretics and renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibitors

questions.verifiedExplanation: This is a classic presentation of pseudohyperkalemia from in vitro hemolysis. The initial elevated K+ (6.8) with a hemolyzed sample followed by a normal repeat (4.5) confirms that potassium was released from lysed red blood cells during specimen collection or processing — NOT from true extracellular potassium elevation. Red blood cells contain approximately 140 mEq/L of potassium intracellularly; lysis of even a small number of RBCs can significantly elevate the measured serum K+. Common causes of hemolyzed specimens: traumatic phlebotomy, small-gauge needle, excessive vacuum, vigorous mixing, prolonged tourniquet application, or pneumatic tube transport. The normal repeat draw confirms the patient's true potassium is normal. Medication effects do not 'wear off' between two rapid draws. The kidneys cannot correct hyperkalemia that rapidly. Acute transcellular shifts would not resolve spontaneously in minutes.
verifiedReferences: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: distinguish true potassium elevation from pseudohyperkalemia

questions.verifiedExplanation: Emergent hemodialysis is the definitive treatment for refractory hyperkalemia (K+ 7.5 despite medical management). Hemodialysis directly removes potassium from the blood and is the most effective method of potassium elimination. It can lower serum K+ by 1-1.5 mEq/L per hour. While high-dose loop diuretics can enhance renal K+ excretion, they are ineffective in patients with significant renal failure (which is the most common cause of refractory hyperkalemia). Repeated insulin/glucose boluses provide only temporary intracellular shifting — potassium will redistribute back to the extracellular space once insulin effect wanes. IV calcium gluconate stabilizes the cardiac membrane but does NOT remove potassium. When medical management fails, dialysis is the definitive intervention.
verifiedReferences: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Patients with severe renal failure and treatment-resistant hyperkalemia underwent hemodialysis

questions.verifiedExplanation: Metabolic acidosis commonly causes hyperkalemia through the H+/K+ exchange mechanism: excess hydrogen ions (H+) move INTO cells to be buffered, and potassium (K+) moves OUT of cells to maintain electroneutrality. This transcellular shift raises serum K+ without changing total body potassium. StatPearls confirms: 'Metabolic acidosis may cause intracellular potassium to shift into the extracellular space without red cell injury.' This is clinically critical in DKA, where insulin deficiency + acidosis cause hyperkalemia despite total body K+ depletion. Metabolic ALKALOSIS causes hypokalemia (opposite direction of shift). Respiratory acidosis does not increase GI K+ absorption. Respiratory alkalosis does not cause direct tubular K+ retention — it can cause mild hypokalemia through intracellular shift.
verifiedReferences: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Metabolic acidosis may cause intracellular potassium to shift into the extracellular space without red cell injury
